title: Putting Veterans First Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Putting Veterans First Act of 2025 protects veterans and their families from job cuts and harmful policy changes. The bill brings back any Department of Veterans Affairs workers who were fired after January 20, 2025, and stops the Department of Government Efficiency from looking at private veteran information. It also requires the government to write detailed reports about how any changes might hurt veteran services. This law makes sure veterans get the help and services they deserve.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Putting Veterans First Act of 2025. The law includes many different sections that are listed in a table of contents. The main parts cover putting veteran and military families first, protecting Department of Veterans Affairs employees, protecting civil servants, providing mental health care for civil servants, helping civil servants find jobs, limiting the Department of Government Efficiency, meeting the financial needs of the Department of Veterans Affairs, and requiring certain reports.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: This section explains what important words mean in this law. An agency means a government department or office. A caregiver is an adult family member or other person who helps take care of a wounded, sick, injured, or disabled military member or veteran or their family member who has health problems. Civil service means government jobs that are not military jobs. Demotion means being moved to a lower job level. A member of the armed forces is someone serving in the military. A military spouse is the husband or wife of someone in the military, or the widow or widower of someone who died while serving. Removal means being fired from a government job. Reserve component means the part-time military like the National Guard. Suspension means being placed on leave without pay for more than seven days. A survivor is a family member of a military member or veteran who died while serving or after retirement from a service-connected injury or illness. A veteran is someone who served in the military and was discharged, except for those who received a dishonorable discharge or were dismissed by a court-martial.

sections.title: Title I  Putting Veteran and Military Families First
sections.text: This title includes four sections. Section 101 brings back any veterans, military spouses, caregivers, survivors, or reserve members who were fired, demoted, or suspended from government jobs between January 20, 2025 and when this law is passed. These actions will have no effect and the people will get back pay and benefits. They can choose to resign if they want. Section 102 protects these same groups from being fired, demoted, or suspended in certain ways, like being part of a group of more than five people fired on the same day, or without proper notice to their supervisor. Any such actions must be reviewed by the Merit Systems Protection Board or Office of Special Counsel within ten days. Section 103 requires government agencies to report every three months about how many veterans and military family members they hire, fire, or employ. Section 104 requires the Comptroller General to write a report by April 1, 2027 about how the federal government employs military community members.

sections.title: Title II  Department of Veterans Affairs Employees
sections.text: This title has three parts. Part A protects Department of Veterans Affairs employees by limiting hiring freezes unless they save money, requiring one year notice before closing offices, requiring one year notice before changing work-from-home policies, and preventing the Department from canceling job offers without good reason. Part B requires reporting, including giving 180 days notice before layoffs, improving personnel reporting on a public website, reporting on research employees who were terminated, and preventing the Veterans Affairs Secretary from also leading other federal agencies. It also protects the independence of oversight offices. Part C requires restoring programs and services by requiring a report on what was removed since January 20, 2025, bringing back any employees who were fired and any programs that were cancelled, restoring websites and materials, and withdrawing a directive about flags at Veterans Affairs facilities.

sections.title: Title III  Protections for Civil Servants
sections.text: This title provides four protections for government employees. Section 301 requires the President to nominate someone to fill any vacancy on the Merit Systems Protection Board within thirty days. Section 302 gives probationary employees (those in their first year) the right to appeal if they are fired. Section 303 allows employees to cancel any agreement they made to resign later without penalty. Section 304 prevents moving jobs from competitive service (where people compete for jobs) to excepted service (where they don't) unless the current employee agrees or the job is empty, and requires two years notice to Congress and the employee before making such changes.

sections.title: Title IV  Mental Health Care for Civil Servants
sections.text: This title provides mental health support for government workers. Section 401 requires agencies to pay for mental health services for veterans, military spouses, caregivers, survivors, or reserve members who are fired, demoted, or suspended after January 20, 2025. The agency must pay for ninety days of mental health care starting from when the person lost their job. Section 402 requires the Department of Veterans Affairs to send a mobile counseling center to any agency that fires, suspends, or demotes more than five military community members on any day. It also prevents agencies from reducing funding for mental health and employee assistance programs below January 5, 2025 levels until February 1, 2030.

sections.title: Title V  Employment Assistance for Civil Servants
sections.text: This title helps military community members find new jobs. Section 501 requires the President to order measures to help veterans, military spouses, caregivers, survivors, and reserve members who were fired, demoted, or suspended after January 20, 2025 find new employment. Section 502 requires the Office of Personnel Management and Department of Labor to work together to expand federal hiring programs for military community members, develop partnerships with private companies to create job opportunities, work with the Chamber of Commerce to form these partnerships, and find ways to include hiring preferences for military community members in government contracts with private companies.

sections.title: Title VI  Department of Government Efficiency
sections.text: This title limits the Department of Government Efficiency's access to Veterans Affairs information. Section 601 prevents anyone who is not a Department of Veterans Affairs employee or contractor from accessing the Department's computer systems, health data, financial systems, or other records unless they meet strict requirements including having proper security clearance, not having conflicts of interest, working in government for at least one year, completing required training, and signing ethics agreements. It also requires deleting any improperly obtained data and removing information technology connections to non-compliant entities within fifteen days. Section 602 prevents four specific Executive Orders about the Department of Government Efficiency from applying to the Veterans Affairs Department. Section 603 requires the Veterans Affairs Secretary to report on compliance within thirty days and requires the Inspector General to review any unauthorized access that occurred between November 6, 2024 and when this law is passed.

sections.title: Title VII  Financial Needs of the Department of Veterans Affairs
sections.text: This title addresses the Department of Veterans Affairs' financial needs. Section 701 defines mass contract cancellation as ending five or more contracts in one day or ten or more contracts in five days, but allows canceling individual contracts for poor performance or fraud. Section 702 requires quarterly reports until September 30, 2029 on costs from Department of Government Efficiency changes, including contract cancellation costs, wrongful termination settlements, and hiring needs. Section 703 requires bringing back all contracts cancelled in mass cancellations between January 20, 2025 and when this law passes, pausing any ongoing mass cancellations, and not starting new ones without meeting specific conditions including reviewing all cancelled contracts and certifying that canceling them won't affect veteran services. Section 704 requires thirty days notice to Congress before any mass contract cancellation and detailed reporting on contracts cancelled. Section 705 requires sixty days notice before changing the Department's charge card program.

sections.title: Title VIII  Reporting Requirements
sections.text: This title requires several reports to track Veterans Affairs performance. Section 801 requires the Veterans Benefits Administration to publish a workload report every Monday showing how many claims are pending, how long they take to process, and other data about processing veteran benefit claims. Section 802 improves reporting on appeals by requiring information about appeal outcomes broken down by type of review and where they came from. Section 803 requires publishing wait times for veterans to get care from community providers outside the Veterans Affairs system, updated at least weekly. Section 804 requires the Veterans Affairs Secretary to respond to questions from Veterans Affairs Committee members within forty-five business days for formal questions, fifteen business days for information requests and letters, with advance notice if delays are expected.
